content: Vaccines prime adaptive immunity by presenting antigenic epitopes—via attenuated pathogens, inactivated components, subunit proteins, or nucleic acid platforms—that are internalized and processed by antigen-presenting cells, predominantly dendritic cells. These APCs migrate to secondary lymphoid organs where they present peptides on MHC class I and II molecules to naive CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, respectively, initiating clonal expansion and differentiation into cytotoxic, helper, and memory subsets. Concurrently, B cells recognizing native antigen undergo activation, aided by follicular helper T cells within germinal centers, driving somatic hypermutation and class-switch recombination to produce high-affinity, isotype-switched antibodies. The resultant immunological memory—comprising long-lived plasma cells and memory T and B cells—enables rapid, robust secondary responses upon pathogen re-exposure. This orchestrated process not only confers protection without the morbidity of natural infection but also informs rational vaccine design by harnessing mechanistic insights into antigen processing, lymphocyte activation, and durable memory formation.
